2. when everything is in the right place or everybody is doing the right thing: Our teacher likes order in the classroom - Are these papers in order (= correct and tidy)?

an idea, belief, or understanding of something
to combine two or more separate things into one, often confusing them
Something that is definite is clear, fixed and unlikely to change (SAME MEANING: certain): I want a definite answer, 'yes' or 'no'

1. to hold something and take it to another place or keep it with you: He carried the suitcase to my room - I can't carry this box _ it's too heavy - Do the police carry guns in your country?
2. to move people or things: Special fast trains carry people to the city centre
`carry on` to continue: Carry on with your work - If you carry on to the end of this road, you'll see the post office on the right
`carry out something` to do or finish what you have planned: The bridge was closed while they carried out the repairs

1. a word that we use to talk about choosing between two things: I don't know whether to go or not
2. if: she asked me whether I was Spanish
a ship that can travel underwater

1. to go down quickly towards the ground: The book fell off the table - She fell down the stairs and broke her arm
2. (also fall over) to suddenly stop standing: He slipped on the ice and fell - I fell over and hurt my leg
3. to become lower or less: In the desert the temperature falls quickly at night - Price have fallen again (OPPOSITE: rise)
`fall apart` to break into pieces: The chair fell apart when I sat on it
`fall asleep` to start sleeping: She fell asleep in the armchair
`fall behind` to become slower than others, or not do something when you should do it: She's falling behind with her school work
`fall for somebody` to begin to love somebody: He has fallen for someone he met on holiday
`fall in love with somebody` to begin to love somebody: He fell in love with Anna the first time they met
`fall out with somebody` to argue with somebody so that you stop being friends: Jane has fallen out with her best friend (OPPOSITE: make up)
`fall through` if a plan falls through, it does not happen
to be classified or included in a category, group, or heading
something that exists independently as a separate unit
to give something to somebody, especially in a formal ceremony: The prizes were presented to the winners -They presented their teacher with some flowers
providing useful knowledge or information

1. to tell somebody what they must do: He instructed the driver to wait
2. (formal) to teach somebody something: Children must be instructed in road safety
separated by a large distance or difference
to say a piece of writing or a poem aloud from memory or by reading
to make a connection between things or people in your mind: Most people associate Austria with snow and skiing - These illnesses are associated with smoking
breed of dog
a kind of animal: There are many different breeds of dog
(also informal till) up to a certain time or event: The shop is open until 6.30 - Stay in bed until you feel better - I can't come until tomorrow
limited to only one person or group; not shared
model, framework, pattern

1. a change in what people think about something: There has been a shift in public opinion away from the war
2. a group of workers who begin work when another group finishes: the night shift
a major change in how people think about something
to show someone where they should go, or to make someone go where you want them to go: She ushered us into her office and offered us coffee
a particular way of thinking about something
large from one side to the other (SAME MEANING: wide): a broad river (noun: breadth) (OPPOSITE: narrow)

1. to need somebody or something (SAME MEANING: depend on somebody or something): I rely on my parents for money
2. to feel sure that somebody or something will do what they say they will do: You can rely on him to help you (adjective: reliable)
to change a person or thing completely: Electricity has transformed people's lives
to make an action or process easier or possible

later in a process or pipeline. It refers to steps that come after some initial processing: First we extract features from the image. Then a downstream classifier uses those features to recognize the digit
result
consist of something to be made from two or more things; to have things as parts: Jam consists of fruit and sugar

1. sweet food made from fruit and suga. You eat jam on bread: a jar of strawberry jam
2. a situation in which you cannot move because there are too many people or vehicles
for example

1. to move towards the person who is speaking or the place that you are talking about: Come here, please - The dog came when I called him - Here comes Colin (= Colin is coming) - I'm sorry, but I can't come to your party
2. to arrive at or reach a place: if you go along that road, you will come to the river - A letter came for you this morning
3. to go somewhere with the person who is speaking: I'm going to a party tonight. Do you want to come with me?
4. to be in a particular position: June comes after May
`come about` to happen: How did this situation come about?
`come across something` to find something when you are not looking for it: I came across these old photos yesterday
`come apart` to break into pieces: This old coat is coming apart
`come back` to return: What time will you be coming back?
`come down` to fall or become lower: The price of oil is coming down
`come from somewhere or something` 1. The place that you come from is where you were born or where you live: I come from Japan - Where do you come from?
2. to be made from something or produced somewhere: Wool comes from sheep
`come in` to enter a place: Come in and sit down
`come off something` to become removed from something: The handle has come off this cup
`come on!; come along` words that you use for telling somebody to hurry or to try harder: Come on! We'll be late!
`come out` to appear: The rain stopped and the sun came out - His first novel came out in 2004
`come round` to visit a person at their house not very far away: Come round for lunch on Saturday
`how come ...?` (informal) why or how ...?: How come you're here so early?
`to come` in the future: You'll regret it in years to come
`come up with something` to suggest or think of an idea or plan: She's come up with some amazing scheme to double her income
to say how much you think something will cost, how big something is, or how long it will take to do something: The builders estimated that it would take a week to repair the roof

1. the part along the end or side of something: She stood at the water's edge - the edge of the table
2. the sharp part of a knife or tool
`on edge` nervous or worried

1. to put something inside a letter or package: I enclose a cheque for $100
2. to put something, for example a wall or fence, around a place on all sides: The prison is enclosed by a high wall
an empty space or opening in something: I'm going to dig a hole in the garden - My socks are full of holes
the way that someone or something is shown or described
vs; used to compare two different things or choices: private education versus state education
to make something better by removing mistakes or making it more exact
doing something again and again, usually to improve it or to get closer to a goal: iterative processes

1. two things of the same kind that you use together: a pair of shoes
2. a thing with two parts that are joined together: a pair of glasses
3. two people or animals together: a pair of ducks
`in pairs` with two things or people together: Shoes are only sold in pairs - The students are working in pairs
many times; happening a lot

1. to insert a substance or material into the body, often through a needle

2. to add or insert something new or different into a situation, system, or activity: inject data into a system

1. it does not matter how: I never win, however hard I try
2. but: She's very intelligent. However, she's quite lazy
3. a way of saying 'how' more strongly
to take something (e.g., data or food) into a system

1. the most important part or idea, from which something grows: Her notes formed the basis of a book
2. the way something is done or organized: We meet on a regular basis (= often)
to make something by hand; in ML, features/rules created by humans rather than learned
to find or learn something for the first time: Who discovered Australia? - I was in the shop when I discovered that I did not have any money
causing major change that replaces old ways of doing things
to be the reason why something happens: What caused the accident? - The fire was caused by a cigarette

1. to take a person or an animal somewhere by going with them or in front of them: He led me to the classroom
2. to go to a place: This path leads to the river
3. to make something happen: Smoking can lead to heart disease
4. to have a particular type of life: They lead a very busy life
5. to be the first or the best, for example in a race or game: Who's leading in the race?
6. to control a group of people: The team was led by Gwen Hollis
relating to a system that is organized based on social ranking or levels of authority

used for showing that someone or something belongs to or is connected with a particular person or thing: Is that your own camera or did you borrow it? - I have my own room now that my sister has left home - I want a home of my own
a view of somebody or something that is quick and not clear: I caught a glimpse of myself in the mirror

1. any or every; anything or everything: These animals eat whatever food they can find - I'll do whatever I can to help you
2. it does not matter what: Whatever you do, don't be late
3. a word that you say to show that you do not mind what you do or have: 'What shall we do tomorrow?' 'Whatever'

1. to feel unhappy or angry about something: Do you mind if I smoke? - I don't mind the heat at all
2. to be careful of somebody or something (SAME MEANING: watch): Mind the step
`do you mind ...?; would you mind ...?` please could you ...?: It's cold _ would you mind closing the window?
`I don't mind` it is not important to me which thing: 'Do you want tea or coffee?' 'I don't mind'
`mind out` used to tell somebody to get out of the way or to be careful: Mind out! There's a dog in the road
`never mind` don't worry; it doesn't matter: 'I forgot your book' 'Never mind, I don't need it today'
in a way or by some method that is not known or certain
to get something, often with difficulty: Where can I obtain tickets for the play
a feeling of wanting something very much: a desire for peace
a standard by which you judge, decide about, or deal with something

1. to have or to form an opinion about somebody or something: It's difficult to judge how long the project will take
2. to decide who or what wins a competition: The headmaster judged the painting competition
to give something to somebody who needs it: I'll provide the food for the party - The company have provided me with a car
a difference between two things that should be the same: There is some discrepancy between the two accounts

1. a formal agreement between two or more people, groups, organizations, or countries

2. behavior and actions that most members of a society expect and consider appropriate
changing or progressing in small, steady steps rather than in sudden leaps or bounds
to make partial or small changes to something, especially to improve it
to successfully reach a goal or obtain something through effort
to say something strongly to show that it is important (SAME MEANING: stress): She emphasized the importance of hard work

1. easy to see, for example because it is bigger than usual: prominent teeth
2. important and famous: a prominent writer
shown to be true (SAME MEANING: sure)
having passed the exams or done the training necessary to do a particular job: Tim is now a qualified architect
the situation within which something exists or happens, and that can help explain it
to make a system or process simpler and more efficient

1. words that you use to tell or ask somebody what is the right thing to do (SAME MEANING: should): It's late _ you ought to go home
2. words that you use to say what you think will happen or what you think is true (SAME MEANING: should): Tim has worked very hard, so he ought to pass the exam
to connect or convert one thing (like an input) into another (like an output): f(x) = x², so f maps x to x² - The neural network maps an image to a label
having more than two dimensions (for example, a 3D or 4D array)

1. an important part of something: Pictures are a feature of this dictionary
2. one of the parts of your face, for example your eyes, nose or mouth: Her eyes are her best feature
3. a newspaper or magazine article or programme about something: The magazine has a special feature on education
to make something happen faster or more quickly
to give all or most of one's time, effort, or resources to a particular activity, cause, or person

1. useful, easy or quick to do; not causing problems: Let's meet on Friday. What's the most convenient time for you? (OPPOSITE: inconvenient)
2. near to a place or easy to get to: The house is very convenient for the station (OPPOSITE: inconvenient)

1. Parallel lines have the same distance from each other at every point

2. happening at the same time; in computing, doing many calculations at once
to make somebody want to do something: The best teachers know how to motivate children to learn

1. able to speak easily and correctly: Ramon is fluent in English and French
2. spoken easily and correctly: She speaks fluent Arabic
able to be reached or easily obtained

1. to arrive somewhere: It was dark when we reached Paris - Have you reached the end of the book yet?
2. to put out your hand to do or get something: I reached for the telephone
3. to be able to touch something: Can you get that book from the top shelf for me? I can't reach

words.mean_fa: عملی، تجربی، مبتنی بر انجام کار با دست

words.mean_en:
1. to say something strongly to show that it is important: I must stress how important this meeting is
2. to say one word or part of a word more strongly than another: You should stress the first part of the word 'happy'
the process of getting or learning something, such as skills, knowledge, or assets
to get all the things that are needed for doing something: Before setting out, they equipped themselves with a map - The kitchen is well equipped
a line of connected bones going down from your neck to tail bone in the middle of the back

to speak in an honest way without trying to be kind: My boss didn't pull any punches during my review; she told me I was doing a terrible job - I'm going to not pull any punches here: you need to study much harder if you want to pass
ready and happy to do something: I'm willing to work at weekends (OPPOSITE: unwilling)
to jump into water, especially with your head and arms going in first, or to move down under the water: They ran to the pool, dived in, and swam to the other side
A sketch is a drawing that is done quickly without a lot of details. Artists often use sketches as a preparation for a more detailed painting or drawing

1. to have something as a part: The job involves using a computer
2. if you involve somebody in something, you make them take part in it: I want to involve more people in the concert
to finish or complete something in a good or satisfying way; to bring something to a pleasant conclusion
to appear or happen, especially suddenly or unexpectedly

1. books, plays and poetry; written works that are valued as works of art, such as novels, plays and poems: He is studying English literature

2. (in scientific context) all the published research and writing on a particular subject
(a lot of objects in) a state of being untidy: Sorry about the clutter in the kitchen
to show something that is usually covered or hidden: A baby's skin should not be exposed to the sun for too long - The newspaper exposed his terrible secret
to show or explain something using examples or pictures

1. to change the way that you do things because you are in a new situation: He has adapted very well to being at a new school
2. to change something so that you can use it in a different way: The car was adapted for use as a taxi
working well without making mistakes or wasting energy: Our secretary is very efficient - an efficient way of working (OPPOSITE: inefficient)
to happen: The accident occurred this morning
the fact of being in a particular place; the existence of something: an experiment to test for the presence of oxygen
able to be changed; likely to change; not consistent
warning
that you know well: I heard a familiar voice in the next room - I'm not familiar with this computer (OPPOSITE: unfamiliar)
the same in some ways but not completely the same: Rats are similar to mice, but they are bigger - Jane and her sister look very similar

1. one complete thing or group that may be part of something larger: The book has twelve units
2. a measurement: A metre is a unit of length and a kilogram is a unit of weight
to produce or provide something, such as a result, profit, or benefit: pytorch provides accelerated computation using graphical processing units (GPUs), often yielding speedup
an increase in speed
